content_markdown:
The average intensity of the solar radiation incident on a planet is $200\text{ W m}^{-2}$. The albedo of the planet is $0.6$. The average temperature of the planet is constant.

Which of the following is a correct statement about the intensity of radiation reflected and radiated by the planet?

diagram_metadata.description: A table showing four options (A, B, C, D) with two columns: 'Intensity reflected by planet' and 'Intensity radiated by planet'.

options.label: A
options.text: Intensity reflected: $120\text{ W m}^{-2}$; Intensity radiated: $80\text{ W m}^{-2}$

options.label: B
options.text: Intensity reflected: $120\text{ W m}^{-2}$; Intensity radiated: less than $80\text{ W m}^{-2}$

options.label: C
options.text: Intensity reflected: $80\text{ W m}^{-2}$; Intensity radiated: $120\text{ W m}^{-2}$

options.label: D
options.text: Intensity reflected: $80\text{ W m}^{-2}$; Intensity radiated: less than $120\text{ W m}^{-2}$

vibe_explanation: Albedo is the ratio of reflected radiation to incident radiation. With an albedo of $0.6$, the reflected intensity is $0.6 \times 200 = 120\text{ W m}^{-2}$. Since the planet's temperature is constant, it must be in thermal equilibrium, meaning the total power in equals the total power out. The absorbed intensity is $200 - 120 = 80\text{ W m}^{-2}$. To maintain a constant temperature, the planet must radiate away exactly what it absorbs, which is $80\text{ W m}^{-2}$.
